assessment_prompt: Open the prepared trace search, choose one slow trace, and select the slow span.
title: Search latency spikes after a catalog rollout
objective: Find the slow trace and span.
hint_1: In Jaeger, click the trace with the longest colored line.
hint_2: Inside that trace, pick the span with the longest bar.
answer: catalog-api / broad Meilisearch query after a Redis cache miss

assessment_prompt: Open the prepared trace search, choose one slow trace, and select the slow span.
title: Checkout slows while payment still succeeds
objective: Find the slow trace and span.
hint_1: In Jaeger, click the trace with the longest colored line.
hint_2: Inside that trace, pick the span with the longest bar.
answer: inventory-api / repeated stock lookup queries during reserve

inventory-api.query_text: select warehouse_id, quantity, reserved from stock_levels where sku = $1 and warehouse_id = $2

assessment_prompt: Open the prepared trace search, choose one slow trace, and select the slow span.
title: Order history drags for active customers
objective: Find the slow trace and span.
hint_1: In Jaeger, click the trace with the longest colored line.
hint_2: Inside that trace, pick the span with the longest bar.
answer: orders-api / expensive sort in order-history query

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Open the trace links, pick the slow traces and the one healthy trace, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
title: Some search requests stay healthy while others regress
objective: Classify the traces as slow or healthy, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
prompt: Open each trace and decide which ones are fast and which ones are slower.
hint_1: Click the Open trace link below each trace label, then inspect the timing in Jaeger to confirm which traces are slow.
hint_2: After classifying the traces, identify the service that stays slow in the slow traces and explain why it is slow.
answer: catalog-api / broad Meilisearch query after a Redis cache miss

assessment_prompt: Open the trace links, pick the slow traces and the one healthy trace, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
title: Only some checkout traces show the inventory slowdown
objective: Classify the traces as slow or healthy, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
prompt: Open each trace and decide which ones are fast and which ones are slower.
hint_1: Click the Open trace link below each trace label, then inspect the timing in Jaeger to confirm which traces are slow.
hint_2: After classifying the traces, identify the service that stays slow in the slow traces and explain why it is slow.
answer: inventory-api / repeated stock lookup queries during reserve

inventory-api.query_text: select warehouse_id, quantity, reserved from stock_levels where sku = $1 and warehouse_id = $2

assessment_prompt: Open the trace links, pick the slow traces and the one healthy trace, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
title: Order-history traces are mixed after a query-plan change
objective: Classify the traces as slow or healthy, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
prompt: Open each trace and decide which ones are fast and which ones are slower.
hint_1: Click the Open trace link below each trace label, then inspect the timing in Jaeger to confirm which traces are slow.
hint_2: After classifying the traces, identify the service that stays slow in the slow traces and explain why it is slow.
answer: orders-api / expensive sort in order-history query

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Open Jaeger Compare with one before trace and one after trace, compare what changed, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
title: Search changed after a broader query rollout
objective: Compare before and after search traces to confirm what changed.
prompt: The search team suspects a new rollout changed query behavior. Open Jaeger Compare, load a before trace against an after trace, and identify the service and failure mode that changed between them.
hint_1: Use the before trace in Compare as your baseline, then inspect how the search branch behaves in the after trace.
hint_2: The after trace keeps the request open on the catalog search branch far longer than the baseline trace does.
answer: catalog-api / broader Meilisearch query after the change

assessment_prompt: Open Jaeger Compare with one before trace and one after trace, compare what changed, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
title: Checkout changed after an inventory reservation deploy
objective: Compare before and after checkout traces to confirm what changed.
prompt: A reservation deploy may have changed checkout behavior. Open Jaeger Compare, compare a before trace against an after trace, and identify the service and failure mode that changed across the two windows.
hint_1: Use the before checkout trace in Compare to see the healthy shape first, then inspect how the inventory branch expands in the after trace.
hint_2: The after trace repeats inventory work several times before payment starts. The baseline trace does not.
answer: inventory-api / repeated stock lookup queries after the change

inventory-api.query_text: select warehouse_id, quantity, reserved from stock_levels where sku = $1 and warehouse_id = $2

assessment_prompt: Open Jaeger Compare with one before trace and one after trace, compare what changed, then name the responsible service and failure mode.
title: Order history changed after a sort tweak
objective: Compare before and after history traces to confirm what changed.
prompt: The history team suspects a sort change degraded performance. Open Jaeger Compare, compare one before trace against one after trace, and identify the service and failure mode that changed.
hint_1: Use the before trace in Compare to learn the healthy timing pattern before you inspect the after trace.
hint_2: The after trace spends much longer in the order-history query span. That is the change you need to explain.
answer: orders-api / expensive sort after the query change

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Submit the responsible service, the failure mode, the culprit span, and the supporting attribute that proves it.
title: Search slowdown needs span-level proof
objective: Use a supporting span attribute to prove the search diagnosis.
prompt: You already know how to find the slow span. This time you must also prove the diagnosis with a supporting attribute on that span.
hint_1: Find the slow span first. Only then decide which attribute on that span proves what changed.
hint_2: The search branch carries a lab-specific query label you can use as evidence once you have the right span.
answer: catalog-api / catalog.search.fetch_results proven by its span attribute

assessment_prompt: Submit the responsible service, the failure mode, the culprit span, and the supporting attribute that proves it.
title: History slowdown needs span-level proof
objective: Use a supporting span attribute to prove the history diagnosis.
prompt: You already know how to find the slow span. This time the coach expects proof: identify the culprit span and the attribute that anchors the diagnosis.
hint_1: Do not start with attributes. Find the slow history span first, then inspect its tags.
hint_2: The right span carries a lab query label that names the heavy history query.
answer: orders-api / orders.history.load_page proven by its span attribute

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20

assessment_prompt: Submit the responsible service, the failure mode, the culprit span, and the supporting attribute that proves it.
title: Checkout failures need span-level proof
objective: Use a supporting span attribute to prove the payment-path diagnosis.
prompt: Checkout now fails in the payment path. You must identify the culprit span and the attribute that proves the database behavior behind the failure.
hint_1: Ignore successful catalog and inventory work. Find the slow failing span on the payment branch first.
hint_2: The payment span carries a lab-specific failure attribute that tells you what kind of wait happened.
answer: payments-api / lock wait timeout proven by the failure attribute

assessment_prompt: From the intermittent candidate set, select every failing trace, then name the responsible service and issue.
title: Search only regresses on some requests
objective: Identify every slow trace in an intermittent search failure set.
prompt: Search only slows down on some requests after the latest catalog update. Inspect the mixed trace set and select every slow request before naming the responsible service.
hint_1: Do not assume every search trace is bad. Compare the candidate traces and decide which ones actually show the regression.
hint_2: The slow traces keep the catalog search branch open much longer than the healthy traces do.
answer: catalog-api / intermittent broad search query regression

assessment_prompt: From the intermittent candidate set, select every failing trace, then name the responsible service and issue.
title: Checkout only fails on some payment attempts
objective: Identify every failing trace in an intermittent payment failure set.
prompt: Checkout does not fail every time. Inspect the mixed candidate set and select every failing request before you name the service and failure mode.
hint_1: Start by separating the successful checkout traces from the ones that fail on the payment branch.
hint_2: Only the failing traces show the slow payment span ending in the lock-wait behavior.

assessment_prompt: From the intermittent candidate set, select every failing trace, then name the responsible service and issue.
title: Order history only regresses on some requests
objective: Identify every slow trace in an intermittent order-history slowdown set.
prompt: History latency is inconsistent after a query tuning change. Some traces still look healthy. Others are clearly slow. Select every slow trace before naming the responsible service.
hint_1: Do not grade by route alone. Compare the candidate traces and decide which ones actually show the long history query span.
hint_2: Only the slow traces spend heavy time in the history query span before the featured-product lookup begins.

orders-api.query_text: select order_ref, sku, total, status, created_at from orders where user_id = $1 order by total desc, created_at desc limit 20
